key_points: Fractals demonstrate infinite complexity through repeated recursive construction
The perimeter of the Koch Snowflake grows infinitely while the enclosed area remains finite
Nets of solids help convert three-dimensional surface paths into straight lines for shortest path problems
Projection geometry explains how different objects can produce identical 2D views
Isometric drawings preserve proportions of length, width and height using equally inclined axes
examples: After 3 steps of Sierpinski Triangle there are 27 triangles
A pentagonal prism has 10 vertices and 15 edges
Shortest path problems on cubes can be solved by unfolding the cube into a net
Different stacked cube arrangements may produce the same top view
formulas: Vertices in a prism with n-sided base → 2n
Edges in a prism with n-sided base → 3n
Faces in a pyramid with n-sided base → n + 1
exam_tips: Recognize patterns in fractal growth problems
Use nets to analyze surface paths on cubes and cuboids
Understand why different solids can share identical projections
Practice interpreting isometric drawings and converting them to orthographic views
